title: Tunnel book
subtitle: An accordion structure with cut-out windows that create depth
excerpt: A tunnel book is an accordion-folded structure where each leaf has a cut-out window at its centre, creating a receding series of frames when viewed end-on. The effect is a miniature theatre of layered paper planes. Tunnel books have roots in 18th-century peepshow entertainments.
sourceNotes: Tunnel books descend from the 18th-century European peepshow tradition, documented in paper-arts histories of the period. The contemporary bookbinding construction follows the accordion-with-windows method standard in book-arts instruction from the 1990s onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: An accordion-folded book with cut-out windows in each leaf that, when the accordion is extended, creates a receding tunnel-like depth when viewed from the front.
The central cut-out in each leaf of a tunnel book, designed to frame the leaf behind it and create the illusion of depth.

content.text: is a peepshow in a bookbinding form. The accordion is held open at both ends, and the viewer looks through successive

into a receding paper landscape. The deeper the accordion, the more layers and the richer the illusion. The structure is equally at home as a decorative object, a narrative sequence, or a miniature architectural model.

attrs.heading: Cutting tools safety
attrs.body: The window cutting is the most demanding step. Change the blade after every three or four cuts. Cut against a metal ruler on a self-healing mat. Always cut away from the body.

items.name: Cartridge paper or watercolour paper, 150 to 200 gsm
items.notes: One sheet per layer. Five to seven layers gives a convincing depth effect. Heavier paper holds its shape better.

items.name: Craft knife + No. 11 blades
items.notes: For cutting the windows. Replace blades frequently.

items.name: Self-healing cutting mat, A4
items.notes: Work surface for all window cutting.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: For gluing the accordion side strips to each leaf.

items.name: Light box, A4 (optional)
items.notes: Useful for tracing window positions through the layers to check alignment before cutting.

content.text: The front leaf has the largest window. Each successive leaf has a smaller window centred in the same position, so the windows frame each other in perspective. Decide the front window size first (a rectangle leaving a 30 mm border on all sides is a good starting point), then reduce by 8 to 10 mm on each dimension per layer. Sketch the sequence on paper and check by holding sheets in sequence against a light source before cutting.

Mark each window on its leaf with pencil and a ruler. Cut cleanly along the lines with the craft knife, pressing firmly through the paper in a single pass. Remove the waste cleanly. Cut curved or irregular windows freehand in light pencil first, then cut with the knife guided by the pencil line. Erase any visible pencil marks after cutting.

Draw, paint, or collage content onto each layer before assembly. The back leaf is the deepest plane and should carry the focal image. Intermediate layers carry elements that read across multiple planes: a tree on the middle layer reads in front of a horizon on the back leaf and behind a foreground on the front leaf. Work in watercolour, ink, or collage; keep backgrounds lighter and foregrounds darker to reinforce depth.
Make and attach the accordion side strips

The layers are held apart by accordion side strips running down both short edges of the leaves. Cut two strips of 120 gsm card the full height of the leaves and five times the intended leaf separation (typically 25 to 40 mm between layers). Fold each strip into an accordion with sections equal to the leaf separation. Glue one end of each strip to the first leaf and the other end to the last leaf; intermediate layers attach at alternating valley folds. Allow to dry under weight.
Store flat under a light board to prevent the accordion from expanding and bowing the leaves. If displayed open, keep away from direct sunlight to preserve the colours in all the layers.

items.symptom: The windows do not align when the accordion is extended
items.cause: Window centres were not marked from a single reference point on each leaf.
items.fix: On future layers, make a registration template from the back leaf and use it to mark the window centre on every subsequent leaf before cutting.

items.symptom: The accordion side strips are different lengths on the two sides
items.cause: The sections were not measured consistently.
items.fix: Cut both strips from the same strip of card, measuring from the same reference edge.
